questions.question: 1. What is the primary mathematical objective of the K-Means clustering algorithm?
options: A) To maximize the distance between data points within the same cluster
B) To minimize the Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)
C) To find the global median coordinate of the entire dataset
D) To systematically increase the number of dimensions in the feature space

questions.question: 2. Why is feature scaling a critical preprocessing step before applying K-Means?
options: A) It allows the algorithm to natively process categorical text data
B) It automatically detects and removes extreme outliers from the dataset
C) It prevents features with larger numerical ranges from dominating the geometric distance calculations
D) It reduces the number of columns to avoid the curse of dimensionality

questions.question: 3. When using the Elbow Method to determine the optimal number of clusters (K), what do the axes of the graph represent?
options: A) X-axis: WCSS (Variance), Y-axis: Number of Clusters (K)
B) X-axis: Number of Iterations, Y-axis: Centroid Movement
C) X-axis: Number of Clusters (K), Y-axis: WCSS (Variance)
D) X-axis: Number of Data Points, Y-axis: Distance to Centroid

questions.question: 4. You are clustering a dataset that contains several unresolvable, extreme outliers. Which distance metric and algorithm pairing provides the most stable clusters?
options: A) Euclidean distance with K-Means
B) Cosine similarity with K-Means
C) Jaccard similarity with K-Medians
D) Manhattan distance with K-Medians

questions.question: 5. K-Means typically fails to correctly cluster datasets shaped like interlocking crescent moons or concentric rings. What is the fundamental mathematical reason for this failure?
options: A) It relies on distance radiating from a single center point, forcing spherical cluster boundaries
B) The algorithm requires at least three dimensions to calculate curved boundaries
C) The algorithm cannot calculate WCSS when data points have negative coordinate values
D) Lloyd's algorithm limits the maximum number of iterations, which is too low for complex shapes

questions.question: 6. During the 'Update' step of Lloyd's Algorithm, how are the new centroid coordinates calculated?
options: A) By selecting the actual data point that is closest to the previous centroid
B) By moving the centroid halfway towards the furthest point in the dataset
C) By randomly selecting a new coordinate within the bounding box of the cluster
D) By calculating the mathematical mean of the coordinates of all points currently assigned to that cluster

questions.question: 7. Which of the following best describes how a high Dunn Index is achieved to indicate excellent cluster quality?
options: A) By maximizing the distance between different clusters and minimizing the distance between points within the same cluster
B) By minimizing the distance between different clusters and maximizing the distance between points within the same cluster
C) By ensuring the sum of squared distances to the centroid equals zero for all clusters
D) By increasing the number of clusters (K) until each data point is its own centroid

questions.question: 8. After applying One-Hot Encoding to categorical variables, your dataset expands to 500 features. Why might running standard K-Means directly on this encoded data yield meaningless clusters?
options: A) K-Means cannot mathematically compute Euclidean distances involving the binary values 0 and 1
B) One-Hot Encoding introduces negative values, which causes the WCSS objective function to crash
C) The algorithm will prioritize the categorical features and completely ignore the continuous numerical features
D) Due to the Curse of Dimensionality, the Euclidean distances between almost all points begin to look mathematically identical

questions.question: 9. Which of the following scenarios is NOT a valid mathematical condition for the K-Means algorithm to declare 'convergence' and stop running?
options: A) The predefined maximum number of iterations is reached
B) The coordinates of the centroids stop changing entirely
C) The movement of centroids falls below a user-defined microscopic tolerance threshold
D) The Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS) reaches an absolute value of exactly zero

questions.question: 10. You have a dataset where 99% of customers spend between $10 and $50, but 1% spend over $10,000. If you apply Min-Max Scaling to this feature before clustering, what will be the geometric result for the 99% majority?
options: A) They are distributed evenly between 0 and 1
B) They are compressed into a tiny, indistinguishable range near 0
C) They are shifted to negative values to balance the outlier
D) They become exactly equal to the median value of 0.5

questions.question: 11. What distance metric does K-Medians primarily use to calculate the proximity of data points to the centroid?
options: A) Euclidean Distance
B) Cosine Similarity
C) Manhattan Distance
D) Mahalanobis Distance

questions.question: 12. Why are K-Medians mathematically more robust to extreme outliers than standard K-Means?
options: A) It automatically deletes data points that are too far away from the center
B) The median calculation takes the middle value of a sorted list, ignoring extreme numbers
C) It relies on an expanding radius that encompasses all data points equally
D) It squares the distances, which naturally neutralizes the impact of outliers

questions.question: 13. While K-Means aims to minimize the Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS), what is the primary objective function that K-Medians minimizes?
options: A) Sum of Absolute Deviations (SAD)
B) Maximum Intra-cluster Distance
C) The Gaussian Variance
D) Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)

questions.question: 14. Imagine a 1D dataset with the values: [1, 2, 3, 4, 100]. During the update step, what will be the new centroid coordinate for K-Medians compared to K-Means?
options: A) K-Medians is 22, K-Means is 3
B) Both will update to 3
C) Both will update to 22
D) K-Medians is 3, K-Means is 22
